Hi Katie,

Willow's arrival and departure times have been much more consistent, which has been great!  She is such a polite and lovely young woman, and beautiful with our children.

I will be honest, the feedback from my team is that they are still not feeling a huge amount of 'value add' for our children's learning and development (on top of what they already do each and every day).  They are saying that the sessions feel rather repetitive and it doesn't feel 'exciting and energetic' ...which is what they were looking for.  

We have our final gymnastics session for the term next week, and from there I will get my teaching team to vote on whether they would find it beneficial to continue next term or not.

Thanks so much,

Kindest Regards,

patterns.prevention:
• Audit coach schedules across all centres this week to identify coverage gaps longer than 2 weeks and backfill with rotating coaches to ensure consistency.

• Create a handover protocol requiring at least one session overlap when coaches change, so centres aren't working with unfamiliar staff.

• Brief all coaches on centre transitions (mergers, schedule changes) 48 hours before they occur so they can proactively communicate with families.

• Flag centres with 3+ missed sessions in a term for immediate manager check-in to resolve scheduling or coach-fit issues before cancellations happen.

patterns.prevention:
• Audit coach engagement levels across all active centres weekly—flag any with attendance issues or low energy reports immediately for retraining or replacement.

• Establish backup coach protocols so centres never lose continuity when a primary coach departs; communicate succession plans upfront.

• Review program content and equipment at each centre monthly—ensure activities match age groups and difficulty levels, refresh offerings quarterly.

• Schedule manager check-ins with centres at weeks 2, 6, and 12 of each program to catch satisfaction issues before cancellation.

patterns.prevention:
• Contact centres that switched providers in the last 90 days to understand what the competing provider offered differently (pricing, scheduling flexibility, program range).

• Review scheduling constraints across all active centres and identify sessions that repeatedly conflict with childcare operating hours or staff availability.

• Audit current pricing against competitor rates and assess whether bundling multiple programs or offering flexible time slots could improve retention.

• Schedule quarterly check-ins with centres showing strong engagement (like the 51-session centre) to identify pain points before cancellation requests occur.

Summary of their time with us: 
The first week of sessions was described as going well, with the coach, Mateu  noted for being polite, professional, and on time, and there were no concerns raised at this early stage. As the program moved into December 2025, feedback remained stable and positive, with no additional bookings requested at that point but still no issues identified, reinforcing that the program had been well received from the outset.

In January 2026, minor operational adjustments were made to better suit the centre’s schedule, including trialling session times between 1:00 pm and 2:30 pm, which were later confirmed as workable. This period reflected a practical alignment phase where the service was being fine-tuned to fit the centre’s routine while maintaining strong satisfaction. By February 2026, direct feedback from the centre continued to be highly positive, with Mateus again described as “great” and “amazing,” consistently on time, and with no complaints raised, indicating growing confidence in the program’s reliability.

In March 2026, further internal coordination took place, including a confirmed adjustment to a 2:00 pm start time, showing continued responsiveness to centre needs. Around the same time, broader feedback highlighted very strong satisfaction, with both coaches Mateus Gomes and Patricia (Patsy) Del Valle being praised for their energy, engagement, and ability to connect with children. Staff noted that the children were actively enjoying the sessions, and the coaches were described as having positive, bubbly personalities and being highly attentive and professional.

By April 2026, the program had fully stabilised and was operating at a consistently high level, with explicit feedback confirming that Mateus was engaging well with children and delivering sessions that were both enjoyable and well-received. Staff reiterated that children loved the activities and that the coach was a strong positive influence in the program, with no concerns raised at this stage.

In May 2026, feedback continued to reflect satisfaction with the program, with the centre confirming they were happy with the sessions and the coach, although Aletia was noted as being away at the time. Shortly after, the centre made the decision to cancel the program due to budget constraints rather than any issues with delivery or satisfaction. The final communication acknowledged that the service had been well liked and that both the coach and sessions had been appreciated, with an indication that they may reconnect in future when budget allows.

Overall, the timeline reflects a consistently positive service experience from start to finish, characterised by strong coach performance, high child engagement, smooth operational adjustments, and stable satisfaction, with the only reason for discontinuation being external financial constraints rather than program quality or delivery issues.

Next point of contact: purely budget happy to have us back asap when they have budget

strong feedback regarding the value of the program and Coach Charlotte's development over time. However, there were several opportunities where we could have strengthened the relationship further. Early in the partnership, feedback received on 20 January 2026 indicated that sessions were initially "rocky," with concerns around coach engagement, one-on-one interaction with children, and communication with educators. The positive relationship was maintained throughout the cancellation process, and the centre specifically invited future discussions in the new financial year. Dec 2025 2025, with initial feedback during November and December indicating that children were engaged and enjoying the sessions. Following the Christmas period, sessions resumed in January 2026, with final schedule details confirmed on 20 January. On 28 January 2026, more detailed feedback was received from the centre regarding Coach Mateus, with educators noting that while he was well-liked and engaged positively with children, sessions appeared repetitive, lacked visible progression between age groups, and required stronger structure and preparation. Mini Monstars responded immediately by implementing a support plan, arranging for Coach Patsy to shadow sessions and preparing for a coaching transition. However, Patsy's unexpected unavailability resulted in further disruption, and on 11 February 2026 the centre raised concerns after Mateus attended alone when a transition had been expected. During this period, Mini Monstars also worked closely with the centre regarding Affinity invoicing processes and payment procedures, helping navigate several administrative challenges between February and April. On 30 April 2026, after the centre advised they were experiencing budget pressures, Mini Monstars proposed a significantly reduced session structure and discounted pricing model in an attempt to retain services while reducing costs. Despite these efforts, Melanie advised on 12 and 13 May 2026 that the centre was unable to continue due to budget limitations. Overall, Papilio Early Learning Robina's journey included an initially challenging coaching period that was successfully resolved through decisive action and a coach change, resulting in a highly positive experience under Coach Aisa.
